summary: Jeff Atwood examines the tension between code transparency and intellectual property protection in .NET applications. He acknowledges that .NET's JIT compilation makes reverse engineering trivially easy compared to traditional compiled languages. While Microsoft bundled Dotfuscator with Visual Studio to address this concern, Atwood argues that transparency has actually contributed to .NET's success by enabling developers to diagnose problems through decompilation. He concludes that developers should embrace transparency while implementing basic protections, focusing energy on improving their products rather than building elaborate copy protection schemes.
key_points: JIT-compiled languages like .NET carry metadata that makes reverse engineering significantly easier than traditional compiled code
.NET's openness and ease of decompilation has paradoxically contributed to its success and adoption
Obfuscation is only a 'stopgap' that raises the difficulty bar but cannot truly protect client-side code
Commercial component vendors should use basic obfuscation for trial versions but not invest heavily in protection
Frequent meaningful updates beat customers over the head with onerous security measures as an anti-piracy strategy
money_quotes: I believe you can attribute much of .NET's success to its transparency; it's free, easy to obtain, easy to write, and easy to reverse engineer.
Why pay for a component when you can download it, easily decompile it, and comment out the trial restrictions?
.NET's openness is also an indirect compliment to the open source movement, where 'security through obscurity' is a derogatory slur.
You're a lot more likely to beat would-be pirates through frequent, meaningful updates than you are by bothering your customers with increasingly onerous security measures.
I believe it's best to err on the side of transparency. That buys you a lot more in the long run.
I lock the front door of my house, too. Right after I activate my nuclear-powered laser attack robots.
key_insight: Code transparency provides more long-term value than protection; invest in improving your product rather than elaborate copy protection that ultimately fails anyway.
